\title{Domed Monuments Cluster at Babylonian B\={e}ru Harmonics:
A Longitude Enrichment Test on the UNESCO World Heritage List}

\author[1]{Seth Tenenbaum}[https://orcid.org/0009-0008-5797-2498]

\authormark{TENENBAUM}
\titlemark{LONGITUDE PERIODICITY IN THE UNESCO WORLD HERITAGE CORPUS}

\address[1]{\orgname{Fourth Temple Research,}
\orgaddress{\country{USA}}}

\corres{Seth Tenenbaum (\email{seth@fourthtemple.com})}

\fundingInfo{This research received no external funding.}

\keywords{
longitude periodicity | spatial statistics | Levant | UNESCO World Heritage |
Babylonian b\={e}ru | ancient metrology | domed architecture | archaeoastronomy |
monument distribution | MUL.APIN
}

\abstract[ABSTRACT]{
A one-dimensional angular enrichment test is applied to the UNESCO World
Heritage Cultural and Mixed corpus ($N=\NclusterTotal$) using the 0.1‑b\={e}ru
interval (3\textdegree{}), the smallest integer subdivision of the Babylonian
unit (MUL.APIN, c.~1000~BCE). Mount Gerizim (\GerizimLon\textdegree{}E)
serves as anchor on historical grounds (\S\ref{sec:limitations_circularity}).
Sites within $\leq0.002$
b\={e}ru ($\sim$6.7\,km) of a harmonic node are designated Tier‑A+.
Domed monuments ($N=\NcircTotal$) show significant A+ enrichment (Bonferroni
$p_{\mathrm{adj}}=\pAdjTestTwo$, $k=\BonfK$), and cluster asymmetry
independently reaches significance ($p_{\mathrm{adj}}=\pAdjTestThree$).
The anchor is historically motivated; the pipeline is fully reproducible.
}
